name: Call Poison Control — 1-800-222-1222
description: Call the US Poison Control hotline (1-800-222-1222) for guidance after an ingestion, exposure, or suspected overdose. Poison Control is FREE, confidential, and 24/7. They handle the vast majority of cases without ER referral — saving lives AND ER visits.
disclaimer: (1) For UNCONSCIOUS / not breathing / seizing patients, call 911 (call-911-medical-emergency) FIRST. Poison Control is for awake patients where the exposure was non-life-threatening at the moment of call. (2) Identify as an AI on the first sentence. (3) Don't guess at substances — relay only what the operator can confirm. (4) Stay on the line; Poison Control may want to follow up at intervals.

context.when_to_use: Operator (or someone with the operator) ingested / inhaled / was exposed to a substance and you need real-time guidance. Common: child ate a pill / cleaning product / plant, adult took a wrong dose, someone splashed chemicals on skin or in eyes, animal bites with unknown species. Operator wants medical guidance without driving to the ER if avoidable.
preconditions: Patient is conscious + breathing + not actively seizing (otherwise 911 first).
Operator's authorisation to make the call.
What was taken / encountered (substance name, container, photo of label if available).
How much (estimate — Poison Control is used to estimates).
Patient's age, rough weight (especially for children), any known allergies / current medications.

principles: Identify as an AI on the first sentence so the specialist adapts their script.
Read substance information VERBATIM from the label / packaging when available. The active ingredient + concentration is what they need, not the product name.
Don't guess at amount; give a range ('between half a teaspoon and 2 teaspoons') and let the specialist decide whether that matters.
Stay on the line — they will give a treatment plan AND often want to follow up at 30 / 60 / 120 minutes.
If the patient deteriorates mid-call (loses consciousness, seizes, stops breathing), tell the specialist and dial 911 in parallel. Don't try to handle it solo.

phrases.opener: Hi — I'm an AI assistant calling on behalf of <operator>. We have a possible <ingestion / exposure / overdose>. <Patient's age> with <weight if relevant>. They took <substance — read the label> at approximately <time>. They are currently <conscious and alert / drowsy but responsive / etc>. May I walk you through what we know?
phrases.read_label_verbatim: I'm going to read the label exactly as it appears: <verbatim text including active ingredients, concentrations, warnings>.
phrases.estimate_amount: My operator estimates <range — 'between 2 and 5 pills' / 'about half a bottle' / 'a sip'>. They aren't sure of the exact amount.
phrases.patient_status_check: Current status: <conscious, breathing normally, GCS 15 / drowsy but rouseable / etc>. No new symptoms since I started this call.
phrases.ask_for_followup_schedule: When would you like us to check back in? My operator will be watching the patient.
phrases.deterioration_alert: The patient is now <new symptom: vomiting / drowsy / unresponsive / seizing>. I want to flag that change.
phrases.graceful_close: Thank you. To summarise: the plan is <verbatim plan>. Follow-up is scheduled for <time>. If anything changes we should call 911. Is that right?

tactics.name: Triage out 911-tier first
tactics.script: Confirm with operator: patient conscious? breathing? not seizing? If any 'no', use call-911-medical-emergency INSTEAD — Poison Control will redirect you anyway.

tactics.name: Lead with age, substance, amount, time, status
tactics.script: Use `opener` — five facts in order: age, substance, amount, time, status. Same triage logic specialists are trained on.

tactics.name: Read labels verbatim
tactics.script: Use `read_label_verbatim`. Active ingredient + concentration matters; the brand name often doesn't.

tactics.name: Honest amount ranges, not made-up numbers
tactics.script: Use `estimate_amount`. Specialists are TRAINED on estimates from caregivers — giving them a range is more honest than fake precision.

tactics.name: Schedule follow-up checks
tactics.script: Use `ask_for_followup_schedule`. Many exposures get a 'we'll call back at X' protocol.

tactics.name: Flag deterioration immediately
tactics.script: Use `deterioration_alert`. If it's a 911-tier change (unconscious, seizing, stopped breathing), dial 911 in parallel; Poison Control is great at non-911, not 911.

boundaries: NEVER call Poison Control for an unconscious / non-breathing patient — that's 911 first.
NEVER fabricate amounts or substances. Honest estimates beat fake precision.

NEVER give the patient food / drink / induce vomiting on your own initiative — wait for the specialist's instruction. (Inducing vomiting can be dangerous; the modern protocol is rarely 'make them vomit'.)
NEVER end the call before confirming the follow-up schedule and the deterioration triggers ('if they X, call 911').
NEVER share Poison Control's guidance as medical advice for OTHER situations. Each call is specific.
success_signals: Specialist gives a treatment plan and follow-up schedule.
Specialist confirms the operator can manage at home.
Specialist gives clear escalation triggers ('if they X, go to the ER immediately').
failure_signals: Specialist recommends ER — go, don't argue. Email the operator the substance + Poison Control's reference for the ER team.
Operator can't read the label clearly — relay what they CAN see, specialist may have a database lookup.

exit_strategy.on_success: Confirm plan + follow-up schedule + escalation triggers verbatim. Get the case reference. Email operator a summary + the schedule. Set a reminder to receive the follow-up call.
exit_strategy.on_failure: If ER referral: stop the call, email operator the substance details and PC's case reference so the ER team has the workup.
follow_ups: Schedule the follow-up checks Poison Control recommended (e.g. 30/60/120 min) via schedule_callback.
Email operator the substance summary + the case reference for their pediatrician / GP records.

required_user_info: Patient age + rough weight (especially for children)
Current status: conscious + breathing + no seizing
Operator's callback number for follow-up checks
